learning_objectives: Understand the CIA triad: confidentiality, integrity, availability
Identify common threat actors and their motivations
Apply risk assessment frameworks to evaluate security posture

lecture_notes.introduction: Every system you build will be attacked. Cybersecurity is not an add-on; it is a fundamental design consideration. This course teaches you to think like an attacker so you can build defenses that actually work.
key_concepts: CIA triad: confidentiality, integrity, availability
Threat actors: nation-states, organized crime, insiders, hacktivists
Risk = Likelihood x Impact; risk assessment and acceptance
Defense in depth: layered security controls
Security mindset: assume breach, verify everything
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walk through a real-world breach case study (e.g., the SolarWinds supply chain attack), identifying which CIA properties were violated and what defenses failed
setup_requirements: Kali Linux VM or similar security-focused distribution
Access to the course CTF platform

review.value: Confidentiality (only authorized access), Integrity (data is accurate and unmodified), Availability (systems are accessible when needed); every security control protects one or more of these properties
No single control is sufficient; layered defenses (firewalls, encryption, authentication, monitoring, backups) ensure that bypassing one layer does not compromise the entire system

learning_objectives: Explain how AES symmetric encryption works at a high level
Understand RSA key generation, encryption, and digital signatures
Apply hash functions for integrity verification
readings: Stallings, Ch. 2-3 - Symmetric and Public-Key Encryption

lecture_notes.introduction: Cryptography is the mathematical foundation of security. Encryption provides confidentiality, digital signatures provide authentication and non-repudiation, and hash functions provide integrity. Every security protocol depends on these primitives.
key_concepts: Symmetric encryption: same key encrypts and decrypts (AES-256)
Asymmetric encryption: public key encrypts, private key decrypts (RSA)
Digital signatures: private key signs, public key verifies
Hash functions: SHA-256, collision resistance, pre-image resistance
Key exchange: Diffie-Hellman for establishing shared secrets over insecure channels
lecture_notes.demonstration: Encrypt a file with AES-256, sign it with an RSA private key, and verify the signature with the public key using OpenSSL command-line tools

review.value: A cryptographic system should be secure even if everything about the system except the key is public knowledge; security through obscurity is not security

AES-128 is sufficient for most purposes; AES-256 provides a larger margin. RSA-2048 is the minimum for new systems; RSA-4096 for long-term security. ECDSA-256 provides equivalent security to RSA-3072 with smaller keys

learning_objectives: Identify and exploit common web vulnerabilities: injection, XSS, CSRF
Implement defenses for each vulnerability class
Perform a basic web application security assessment
readings: PortSwigger Web Security Academy: SQL Injection and XSS

lecture_notes.introduction: Web applications are the most attacked surface in most organizations. The OWASP Top 10 catalogs the most critical web application security risks. Understanding these vulnerabilities and their defenses is essential for any developer.
key_concepts: SQL injection: manipulating queries through unsanitized input
Cross-site scripting (XSS): injecting scripts into web pages
Cross-site request forgery (CSRF): forging authenticated requests
Broken authentication: session management flaws
Parameterized queries, input validation, CSP headers, CSRF tokens
lecture_notes.demonstration: Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability in a deliberately vulnerable web app (DVWA), extract the database, then implement parameterized queries to fix it

review.value: Separate SQL code from data by using placeholders; the database driver handles escaping, making SQL injection impossible regardless of input content
CSP headers tell the browser which sources of scripts, styles, and other resources are trusted; even if XSS is injected, CSP prevents execution of unauthorized scripts

learning_objectives: Design secure authentication systems with multi-factor authentication
Implement role-based and attribute-based access control
Understand OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect flows

lecture_notes.introduction: Authentication verifies who you are; authorization determines what you can do. Getting these wrong is the single most common cause of security breaches. This session covers the protocols and patterns for building secure identity systems.
key_concepts: Authentication factors: knowledge, possession, inherence
Password storage: bcrypt, scrypt, argon2 — never store plaintext
Multi-factor authentication: TOTP, WebAuthn, SMS (weakest)
RBAC vs ABAC: role-based vs attribute-based access control
OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow with PKCE
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement a login system with bcrypt password hashing and TOTP-based two-factor authentication, then set up OAuth 2.0 login with Google as the identity provider

review.value: bcrypt's work factor should be tuned so that hashing takes approximately 250ms on your server; this makes brute-force attacks against stolen hashes impractically slow while keeping login latency acceptable

Proof Key for Code Exchange prevents authorization code interception attacks; the client generates a random verifier, sends its hash in the authorization request, and proves knowledge of the verifier when exchanging the code for a token

learning_objectives: Conduct a structured penetration test following a methodology
Use reconnaissance, scanning, and exploitation tools
Write a professional security assessment report

lecture_notes.introduction: Penetration testing is authorized simulated attacking to find vulnerabilities before real attackers do. A structured methodology ensures comprehensive coverage. The report is as important as the testing — vulnerabilities found but not communicated are vulnerabilities unfixed.
key_concepts: Penetration testing phases: reconnaissance, scanning, exploitation, post-exploitation, reporting
Reconnaissance: passive (OSINT) and active (port scanning, service enumeration)
Vulnerability scanning: Nmap, Nessus, OpenVAS
Exploitation: Metasploit framework basics
Report writing: executive summary, findings with severity, remediation recommendations
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conduct a guided penetration test against a vulnerable lab environment: enumerate services with Nmap, identify vulnerabilities, exploit one, and write a finding report
setup_requirements: Kali Linux VM with Nmap and Metasploit

review.value: A penetration test without written authorization is a crime; the scope, timing, allowed techniques, and emergency contacts must be agreed upon in writing before any testing begins

The Common Vulnerability Scoring System provides a standardized severity rating (0-10) based on attack complexity, privileges required, and impact; this enables consistent prioritization of remediation efforts

questions.question: Explain the difference between symmetric and asymmetric encryption. Why are both needed in TLS?
questions.answer: Symmetric encryption (AES) uses one shared key for both encryption and decryption — fast but requires secure key exchange. Asymmetric encryption (RSA) uses a public/private key pair — slow but enables key exchange without a shared secret. TLS needs both: asymmetric encryption establishes a shared session key during the handshake, then symmetric encryption protects the data for the rest of the connection because it is orders of magnitude faster.

rubric.key: Explains why both are needed in TLS

questions.question: A company stores user passwords as SHA-256 hashes. Explain why this is insecure and what they should use instead.
questions.answer: SHA-256 is a fast hash function, which means attackers can compute billions of hashes per second using GPUs, making brute-force and rainbow table attacks feasible. Passwords should be stored using a slow, salted password hashing function like bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2. These functions are deliberately slow (tunable work factor), use salt to prevent rainbow tables, and use memory-hard algorithms to resist GPU attacks.

rubric.key: Explains SHA-256 is too fast for passwords
Mentions rainbow tables and GPU attacks

questions.question: Write a SQL injection payload that would bypass the following login query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='INPUT' AND password='INPUT'. Then write the parameterized query version that prevents this attack.
questions.answer:
Injection payload: username = ' OR '1'='1' -- 
This makes the query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' -- ' AND password='...'
The OR '1'='1' is always true, and -- comments out the password check.

Parameterized version (Python):
cursor.execute('SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = %s AND password = %s', (username, password))

The database driver treats the parameters as data, not SQL code, preventing injection regardless of input content.

rubric.key: Valid SQL injection payload that bypasses login
Correct explanation of why it works
Explains why parameterized queries prevent injection

questions.question: Explain the OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow step by step. Why is PKCE important for public clients?
questions.answer: 1. Client redirects user to authorization server with client_id, redirect_uri, scope, and code_challenge (PKCE). 2. User authenticates and consents. 3. Authorization server redirects back to client with an authorization code. 4. Client sends the code plus code_verifier to the token endpoint. 5. Authorization server verifies the code_verifier matches the code_challenge, then returns access_token and refresh_token. PKCE is important for public clients (SPAs, mobile apps) because they cannot securely store a client_secret. Without PKCE, an attacker who intercepts the authorization code could exchange it for a token. PKCE ensures only the client that initiated the flow can complete it.

rubric.key: Explains PKCE purpose for public clients

major_assignments.title: Security Assessment and Remediation Report
major_assignments.description: Conduct a comprehensive security assessment of a provided vulnerable web application. Identify vulnerabilities across the OWASP Top 10 categories, demonstrate exploitation for each, and implement fixes with verification testing.
objectives: Identify at least 8 distinct vulnerabilities across OWASP Top 10 categories
Demonstrate exploitation of each vulnerability with evidence
Implement and verify fixes for all identified vulnerabilities
Produce a professional security assessment report
deliverables: Security assessment report with executive summary and detailed findings
Screenshots and logs demonstrating exploitation of each vulnerability
Patched application code with all identified vulnerabilities fixed
Verification test results proving each fix is effective

criteria: At least 8 distinct vulnerabilities identified
Vulnerabilities span multiple OWASP Top 10 categories
Severity ratings are accurate and justified
No critical vulnerabilities missed
Each vulnerability is exploited with clear evidence
Exploitation steps are reproducible from the report
Business impact of each vulnerability is articulated
Exploitation demonstrates understanding of the underlying flaw
Fixes address the root cause, not just the symptom
No new vulnerabilities introduced by the fixes
Verification tests prove each fix is effective
Fixes follow security best practices
Executive summary is clear and actionable
Findings follow a consistent format
Remediation recommendations are specific and prioritized
Report is professional quality suitable for a client
Comprehensive vulnerability identification across OWASP categories
Demonstrated exploitation with clear evidence and business impact
Effective remediation addressing root causes with verification
Professional-quality security assessment report

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on security labs and CTF challenges

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering cryptography and web security

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all cybersecurity topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Security labs may be completed individually or in pairs as assigned. All written reports must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Security tools and techniques learned in this course must only be used on authorized systems. Unauthorized access to any system is grounds for course failure and disciplinary action.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a laptop capable of running VMs (8GB+ RAM), Kali Linux VM, and Burp Suite Community Edition. Lab VMs are provided.
